content:
7 min read
1 hour ago

--

A build log about word level highlight syncing, offscreen audio, and every browser security boundary that stood in the way.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Reading a live article aloud, with the current word highlighted in place so your eyes keep their spot.The idea was almost embarrassingly simple. Point at any article, hit a shortcut, and have it read back to me. Not in the flat robotic voice every screen reader defaults to, but something natural, with the words lighting up on the page as they were spoken so my eyes could still follow along.
Then I opened the Manifest V3 documentation and learned that Chrome disagrees with almost every part of that sentence.
The result is Whisperian, a Chrome extension that reads web pages, text selections, and local PDFs aloud, highlighting each block and each word in time with the audio. This isn’t a feature tour. It’s a walk through the architecture, because the genuinely interesting part was never the text to speech. It was everything the browser’s security model did to stop me from playing it.
What I actually wanted
Three reading modes, one experience.

• A whole article. Press Alt + R. Every readable block becomes seekable. Click any paragraph to jump reading straight to it.

• A selection. Highlight a passage, right click, choose Read selection aloud. Only that passage is read.

• A local PDF. Open a file:// PDF and hand it to a built in reader page that renders with pdf.js and behaves exactly like a live web page.
Underneath all three sits the same promise: word level highlighting that tracks the voice, and a floating player that never breaks the page it’s living on.
Easy to describe. Every clause of that description turned into a fight with the browser.
Fight #1: You can’t just play audio
My first assumption was that a content script could fetch an MP3 and call .play(). It can't, not reliably. Chrome's autoplay policy blocks programmatic playback that isn't tied to a user gesture, and plenty of sites ship a Content Security Policy strict enough to smother audio you inject into their context.
The escape hatch in MV3 is the offscreen document: a hidden, extension owned page that runs in your context, not the host site’s. Whisperian spins one up and does all playback there. The host page’s CSP stops mattering because the audio never touches the host page. The autoplay policy relaxes because the extension owns the surface. The service worker manages that document’s lifecycle, creating it on demand and tearing it down when idle, since an offscreen document is a resource you’re expected to be frugal with.
Fight #2: You can’t just call the API
The speech itself comes from Vaani, the TTS backend I run myself, which returns audio plus word level timings. The obvious move is to fetch() it from the content script. Also a dead end: CORS and the host page's CSP will happily block a cross origin request made from inside someone else's page.
So the background service worker becomes a network proxy. Content scripts and the reader page never talk to the API directly. They send a message to the worker, the worker makes the fetch from the privileged extension origin where CORS and CSP don’t apply the same way, and the audio and timestamps come back over chrome.runtime.sendMessage. The same worker does double duty. It routes global keyboard commands to the active tab, registers the Read selection aloud context menu, and relays events to the offscreen document. It ended up being the switchboard the whole extension routes through.
Fight #3: You can’t just highlight text
This one surprised me. The naive way to highlight a spoken word is to wrap it in a <span> and style the span. Do that across a live article and you're mutating the page's DOM hundreds of times a second, triggering reflows, fighting the site's own scripts, and corrupting text selection.
The clean answer is the CSS Custom Highlight API, using Highlight objects and the ::highlight() pseudo element. You register Range objects pointing at the text you want lit up, and the browser paints an overlay without touching the DOM at all. No wrapper elements, no reflow, no collateral damage to the page. Where the API isn't supported, Whisperian falls back to inline styling, but the primary path leaves the document exactly as it found it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Word by word highlighting driven by Vaani’s per word timestamps, painted with the CSS Custom Highlight API so the page’s own DOM is never touched.Fight #4: You can’t trust the page’s CSS
A floating player that injects into arbitrary websites inherits arbitrary CSS. One aggressive * { } rule or a clashing stacking order and your carefully built widget looks broken.
The panel lives inside a Shadow DOM with a closed style boundary, so the host page’s CSS can’t leak in and the panel’s styles can’t leak out. It renders the same on a minimalist blog and on a page that fought me with !important on every selector.
The sync problem
Highlighting the right word at the right moment is the part people actually notice, and it’s where the timestamps earn their keep. Vaani’s /api/tts/timestamps endpoint returns base64 MP3 audio alongside per word start and end times. The reader keeps a playback queue and, as audio time advances, resynchronizes the active highlight against those timings on every update.
The per word timings are the whole reason the highlighting works, and they exist because I control the backend. Most hosted TTS APIs hand you an audio blob and nothing else. Vaani returns start and end times for every word because I built the endpoint to, which is what let the front end treat highlighting as a timeline problem instead of a guessing game.
Two details made it feel smooth instead of stuttery:

• Prefetching. Upcoming chunks are fetched before the current one finishes, so playback is gapless instead of pausing at every paragraph boundary while the network catches up.

• Chunking. The endpoint caps input at 5000 characters, so longer blocks are split in memory before the call and stitched back together on the timeline.
Speed is adjustable from 1x to 2.5x with pitch preserved, which matters more than it sounds. Sped up speech that chipmunks itself is unusable for real reading.
There’s also a quieter piece of UX in the auto scroll. The page follows the active word, but the moment you scroll or interact yourself, it backs off and stops yanking the viewport around. A locate button lets you rejoin the read when you’re ready. Intent detection like that is invisible when it works and infuriating when it doesn’t.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Reusing the whole thing for PDFs
By the time the web page path worked, the PDF feature was almost free. Local PDFs open in a dedicated extension reader page that renders with pdf.js, then converts pdf.js text content into the same ReadableBlock shape the web extractor produces.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Local PDFs open in a dedicated reader page rendered with pdf.js, running the same queue, highlighting, and controls as any web page.From there it reuses the identical Reader, Highlighter, and Panel pipeline. Same queue, same highlighting, same controls. A different source feeding one engine. Designing around a single block abstraction early is the reason a second content source cost a converter and not a rewrite.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Four isolated Chrome contexts talking over chrome.runtime messaging, with Vaani as the self hosted TTS backend and Chrome storage holding settings and PDF handoff state.What I’d tell you if you’re building something similar
A few things I wish I’d internalized before starting.

• Assume Chrome is adversarial to your feature, not neutral. Autoplay, CSP, CORS, and DOM ownership each exist for good reasons, and each one will block a capability that sounds trivial. Design around the boundaries instead of trying to punch through them.

• The offscreen document and the service worker proxy are the two MV3 primitives that unlock most media and network work. Learn them first.

• Never mutate a page you don’t own if you can avoid it. The Custom Highlight API is the difference between an overlay and a bug report from every site with its own text handling.

• Pick your core abstraction before your second feature. One ReadableBlock type is why PDFs, selections, and full pages all run through the same code.
The TTS was the part I thought would be hard. It turned out to be a REST call to a backend I already run. The real engineering was building a reading experience that Chrome’s security model would actually permit, and doing it without leaving a mark on the pages it reads.
Whisperian is built with TypeScript, Vite, and CRXJS on Manifest V3, backed by Vaani, my self hosted TTS engine. If you’re working on anything in the accessibility or hands free reading space, I’d genuinely like to compare notes.
